title: CLAUDE.md
text: socialchange R package decomposing social change components intraindividual change population turnover. package implements multiple decomposition methods analyzing aggregate-level outcomes change time due : - Intraindividual/within-unit changes (e.g., people changing attitudes) - Population turnover (cohort replacement, mortality, migration, coming--age) - Event-driven changes (e.g., countries joining/leaving EU)

title: CLAUDE.md
text: package two main intellectual motivations: Skepticism APC models: package partly critique Age-Period-Cohort (APC) methods. APC models suffer identification problem (age = period − cohort) require untestable assumptions resolve . package offers decomposition-based alternatives directly interpretable. (critique developed future vignettes/papers.) Improving Firebaugh’s CR-IC decomposition: Firebaugh’s algebraic linear CR-IC decompositions classical approach separating cohort replacement intracohort change, known limitations (treatment entering/exiting cohorts, inability handle non-linearities, residuals linear method). package improves methods via model-based counterfactuals (AD+, Model methods cr_ic()) , fundamentally, via simulation-based individual-level decomposition (sim_social_change(), decompose_aggregated()), tracks demographic events directly rather inferring algebraically.

title: CLAUDE.md
text: package implements three main decomposition approaches: decompose_events(): Decomposes change units enter/exit population discrete time points Works two datasets: events data (entry/exit dates) outcomes data (values time point) Formula interface: Outcome ~ Unit + Time Separates change “change” (within-unit) “replacement” (turnover) components Computes counterfactuals showing happen change replacement decompose_aggregated(): data aggregated age covariates Uses microsimulation approach randomly order demographic events Requires: stacked panel data age, period, cell counts Requires: fun_y function computes outcome data Decomposes : intraindividual change, coming--age, mortality, net -migration (last derived residual cell-count growth; see Known Limitations → Migration) Optional population argument supplies true cell × period count frame, overriding survey counts population frame event derivation sim_social_change(): Forward simulation social change dynamics User provides functions : outcome (fun_y), mortality, coming--age, migration, state transitions Returns detailed event--event records showing exact contribution component

title: CLAUDE.md
text: CR-IC decomposition (R/cr_ic.R): Classical cohort replacement vs. intraindividual change decomposition using algebraic, linear, model-based approaches APC models (R/apc.R): Age-Period-Cohort models using orthogonal polynomial contrasts handle identification issues. Also includes plot_gam_surface() visualizing GAM smooths 2D surfaces.

title: CLAUDE.md
text: decompose_aggregated() properly handle within-cell transitions (e.g., smokers becoming non-smokers). function decomposes change : - Intraindividual change - Mortality - Coming--age - Net -migration (residual cell-count growth; see ) input data includes transitions cells, transition effects separately identified instead get absorbed intraindividual change component. Additionally: Mortality, coming--age, net -migration estimated simple population differences periods: survivors mortality = pmax(0, n1 - n2) inmigration = pmax(0, n2 - n1); new cohorts coming_of_age = pmax(0, n2 - n1) transitions occur, differences longer accurately reflect demographic events pmax(0, ...) guard routes cell’s net change exactly one mortality / -migration depending sign, masks offsetting flows Cells net inflow transitions show 0 mortality even deaths occurred (inflow labeled -migration instead) Workaround: fundamental limitation aggregated decomposition approach. simulation function sim_social_change() properly tracks transitions separate component, decompose_aggregated() recover already-aggregated data. practice: Use decompose_aggregated() data without significant within-cell transitions, aware transition effects misattributed intraindividual change.

title: CLAUDE.md
text: current weight normalization approximation revisited. weight provided individual-level data, weights normalized within period sum period sample size (.N), cells get n = round(sum(normalized_weight)). approach: Correctly adjusts relative population structure across cells (e.g., oversampled subgroups get proper share) Keeps event counts (mortality, coming--age) tractable simulation loop Avoids problem population-scaled weights (e.g., inverse probability weights summing millions) making simulation intractable gets wrong: ideal use true population counts per cell per period estimate demographic event magnitudes. Normalized survey weights approximate relative structure don’t recover absolute population sizes. Additionally, rounding introduces small errors, especially cells respondents. Ideal future approach: true population counts (e.g., census official statistics) available alongside survey data, used directly n pre-aggregated input, bypassing weight argument entirely. normalization approach practical stopgap common case survey weights available.

title: Decomposing social change on aggregated data
text: now feed simulated data event-based decomposition: event-based decomposition exactly recovered results.
code: library("data.table")  data <- data.table(     age = c(20:39),     n = c(rep(100, 10), seq.int(100, 10, by = -10)) )  support_for_pensions <- function(data, time) {     data[, (age - 20) * 1 / 20] } coming_of_age <- function(data, period) {     data.table(age = 20, n = 100) } mortality <- function(data, period) {     data[, ifelse(age >= 30, 10, 0)] }  library("socialchange") simresult <- socialchange::sim_social_change(     periods = 5,     data = data,     fun_y = support_for_pensions,     fun_coming_of_age = coming_of_age,     fun_mortality = mortality ) print(simresult, detailed = FALSE) #>                 Component   Value #>  At initial                0.3758 #>  At end                    0.3758 #>  Total change              0.0000 #>  - Intraindividual change  0.2500 #>  - Population turnover    -0.2500 #>    - Mortality            -0.1207 #>    - Out-migration         0.0000 #>    - Coming-of-age        -0.1293 #>    - In-migration          0.0000 stacked_data <- rbindlist(simresult$snapshot) # we know that y depends only on age here model <- lm(y ~ age, data = stacked_data) predict_y <- function(newdata) { predict(model, newdata = newdata) }  decompresult <- decompose_aggregated(stacked_data, predict_y) print(decompresult, detailed = FALSE) #>                 Component   Value Percent #>  At initial (modeled)      0.3758         #>  At end (modeled)          0.3758         #>  Total change              0.0000         #>  - Intraindividual change  0.2500         #>  - Population turnover    -0.2500         #>    - Mortality            -0.1207         #>    - Coming-of-age        -0.1293

code: coming_of_age <- function(data, period) {     data.table(age = c(20, 20), smoking = c("smoker", "nonsmoker"), n = c(90 - period * 15, 10 + period * 15)) }  smoking3 <- socialchange::sim_social_change(     periods = 5,     data = data,     fun_y = smoking_in_public,     fun_coming_of_age = coming_of_age,     fun_mortality = mortality ) print(smoking3, detailed = FALSE) #>                 Component    Value #>  At initial                0.86769 #>  At end                    0.50476 #>  Total change             -0.36293 #>  - Intraindividual change -0.29107 #>  - Population turnover    -0.07186 #>    - Mortality            -0.01048 #>    - Out-migration         0.00000 #>    - Coming-of-age        -0.06138 #>    - In-migration          0.00000 stacked_data <- rbindlist(smoking3$snapshot) model <- lm(y ~ age + period * smoking, data = stacked_data) cor(stacked_data$y, predict(model)) #> [1] 1 predict_y <- function(newdata) { predict(model, newdata = newdata) }  decomp <- decompose_aggregated(stacked_data, predict_y, "smoking") print(decomp, detailed = FALSE) #>                 Component    Value Percent #>  At initial (modeled)      0.86769         #>  At end (modeled)          0.50476         #>  Total change             -0.36293   100.0 #>  - Intraindividual change -0.29099   80.2  #>  - Population turnover    -0.07193   19.8  #>    - Mortality            -0.01068   2.9   #>    - Coming-of-age        -0.06125   16.9

code: # compare to Table 1 in Ekstam -- roughly similar modelsummary::datasummary(   homosex + year + cohort + age + educ ~ mean + SD + min + max,   data = gss_homosex, output = "markdown", fmt = 3) # sex and race are categorical, so show their level breakdown instead modelsummary::datasummary(   sex + race ~ N + Percent(),   data = gss_homosex, output = "markdown", fmt = 1) by_year <- gss_homosex[, list(y = weighted.mean(homosex, wtssall)), by = c("year")] ggplot(by_year, aes(x = year, y = y)) + geom_line() + ylim(0, 1) + theme_light() splinemodel = gam(homosex ~ s(year, cohort), data = gss_homosex) vis.gam(splinemodel, theta = 40) plot_gam_surface(splinemodel)

title: Decomposing General Social Survey data: Attitudes on Homosexuality
text: cr_ic() function implements four methods separating intracohort change (IC) cohort replacement (CR): algebraic decomposition (AD), linear decomposition (LD), two model-based improvements (AD+ Model). detailed explanation method, including replication Firebaugh (1989), see Replications: Firebaugh vignette. Acceptance rose 43 percentage points 1973 2018 (0.19 0.62). Using full panel, two model-based methods attribute larger share intracohort change, though differ exact split: AD+ gives roughly 63% intracohort change 37% cohort replacement, Model method gives roughly 56% 44%.  first last survey years used, balance shifts slightly toward cohort replacement (~54% CR, ~46% IC). illustrates choice time points can affect decomposition results, model-based AD+ Model methods preferred simpler AD.
code: # complete period form <- homosex ~ as.factor(year) + as.factor(cohort) (res <- cr_ic(gss_homosex, homosex ~ year + cohort, weight = "wtssall", model = form)) #> Cohort decomposition (year-over-year) with 26 periods: #>    1973, 1974, 1976, 1977, 1980, 1982, 1984, 1985, 1987, 1988, 1989, 1990, 1991, 1993, 1994, 1996, 1998, 2000, 2002, 2004, 2006, 2008, 2010, 2012, 2014, 2016 #>  #> Summary for entire period: #>       1973      2016 Difference #>      <num>     <num>      <num> #>  0.1893911 0.5642217  0.3748306 #>  #> Decompositions: #>  method factor     value         % #>  <char> <char>     <num>     <num> #>      LD  total 0.3748306 100.00000 #>      LD     IC 0.2062929  55.03632 #>      LD     CR 0.1685376  44.96368 #>      LD  resid 0.0000000        NA #>      AD  total 0.3748306 100.00000 #>      AD     IC 0.2422175  64.62053 #>      AD     CR 0.1326131  35.37947 #>      AD  resid 0.0000000        NA #>     AD+  total 0.3748306 100.00000 #>     AD+     IC 0.2379691  63.48710 #>     AD+     CR 0.1368615  36.51290 #>     AD+  resid 0.0000000        NA #>   Model  total 0.3748306 100.00000 #>   Model     IC 0.2047557  54.62619 #>   Model     CR 0.1700749  45.37381 #>   Model  resid 0.0000000        NA plot(res) # only beginning and end year (also compare to Baunach 2011) form <- homosex ~ as.factor(year) + splines::bs(cohort, 10) (res <- cr_ic(gss_homosex[year %in% c(min(year), max(year))], homosex ~ year + cohort, weight = "wtssall", model = form)) #> Cohort decomposition (year-over-year) with 2 periods: #>    1973, 2016 #>  #> Summary for entire period: #>       1973      2016 Difference #>      <num>     <num>      <num> #>  0.1893911 0.5642217  0.3748306 #>  #> Decompositions: #>  method factor     value         % #>  <char> <char>     <num>     <num> #>      LD  total 0.3748306 100.00000 #>      LD     IC 0.1549435  41.33694 #>      LD     CR 0.2198871  58.66306 #>      LD  resid 0.0000000        NA #>      AD  total 0.3748306 100.00000 #>      AD     IC 0.0764078  20.38462 #>      AD     CR 0.2984228  79.61538 #>      AD  resid 0.0000000        NA #>     AD+  total 0.3748306 100.00000 #>     AD+     IC 0.1923286  51.31080 #>     AD+     CR 0.1825020  48.68920 #>     AD+  resid 0.0000000        NA #>   Model  total 0.3748306 100.00000 #>   Model     IC 0.1961770  52.33750 #>   Model     CR 0.1786536  47.66250 #>   Model  resid 0.0000000        NA

code: gss_all <- gss_homosex[, .(age, period = year, sex, y = homosex, wtssall)] set.seed(42) model <- mgcv::gam(y ~ s(age) + s(period), data = gss_all, weights = wtssall) predict_y <- function(newdata) as.vector(mgcv::predict.gam(model, newdata = newdata)) result <- decompose_aggregated(gss_all, predict_y, weight = "wtssall") print(result, detailed = FALSE) #>                 Component    Value Percent #>  At initial (modeled)     0.200317         #>  At end (modeled)         0.566382         #>  Total change             0.366065   100.0 #>  - Intraindividual change  0.22339   61.0  #>  - Population turnover     0.14277   39.0  #>    - Mortality             0.12283   33.5  #>    - Coming-of-age         0.03215   8.8   #>    - In-migration         -0.01221   -3.3 plot(result) data(wpp_us)  survey_years <- sort(unique(gss_all$period)) pop <- wpp_us[period %in% survey_years] scale_total <- round(mean(gss_all[, .N, by = period]$N)) pop[, n := n / sum(n) * scale_total, by = period]  set.seed(42) result <- decompose_aggregated(gss_all, predict_y, cells = "sex",                                weight = "wtssall", population = pop) print(result, detailed = FALSE) #>                 Component    Value Percent #>  At initial (modeled)      0.19663         #>  At end (modeled)          0.57249         #>  Total change              0.37586   100.0 #>  - text: goal decompose total change two components: intracohort change (IC) cohort replacement (CR), ideally without residual. methods applied GSS attitudes homosexuality GSS homosexuality vignette. document contains analyses based four methods: Algebraic decomposition (AD): problem AD method concerns “entering” “exiting” cohorts. values observed, unclear whether contribute IC CR. Firebaugh assigns change cohorts contribute CR, notes “problematic”. Linear decomposition (LD): Based linear model, therefore take account non-linearities. Also produces residual. AD+: variation AD method accounts entering exiting cohorts treating problem missing data problem: missing values entering/exiting cohort replaced modeled counterfactuals. method strictly better AD method model fit adequate. Model: Taking AD+ step : cohort-specific values replaced modeled values. serves form regularization, can especially useful small cohorts outcome precisely estimated. Additionally, question whether decompose long period year--year. data available, year--year decomposition obvious advantage data used. applies four methods.

title: Replications: Firebaugh
text: (roughly) data analysed Firebaugh (1989) Firebaugh Davis (1988). numbers close reported Firebaugh, though exact match, may reflect differences sample. two-point comparison, LD AD give essentially answer. cr_ic_decomposition object provides useful summary: Using two time points, intracohort change accounts 45% decline according LD 44% according algebraic decomposition. alternative, decomposition can computed year--year, making use available data: LD results largely unaffected, AD now attributes 48% change intracohort change — large difference. package provides plot method shows cumulative IC CR contributions time:  AD+ Model methods require model formula produce counterfactual values. , dummy variables every year cohort used, simpler models (e.g., using splines) also possible. results apply two-time-point dataset. new methods suggest IC > CR. Now using available data: IC component larger methods AD LD, overall conclusion change substantially. CR-IC decompositions work aggregated cohort-level data. granular approach directly tracks demographic events (mortality, coming--age) allows state transitions, see simulation aggregated decomposition vignettes.

title: Simulating social change
text: Building simulation requires three steps: Define population Define outcome function variable interest Define population dynamics (coming age, mortality, - -migration) Step 1: first step define initial population. example, define simple population aged 20 39, uniform distribution age 30, declining age 39. dataset define needs least two columns: age n. can add columns want describe complicated populations, ’ll stick simple setup now. ’ll call row dataset population cell. extreme case, can also define population individuals n just 1. ’s simple population: Step 2: Next, define variable interest want simulate. , define function takes arguments population cells time continuous variable. Time really needs continuous - need know outcome every individual population terms discrete ‘years’ (whatever time scale), every point -well. example, use outcome dependent age: age 20, outcome 0, age 40 outcome 1, values linearly increasing age. purely function age, time, real-world survey, outcome “support investing longevity research” “support increasing pensions” - ’ll just use latter example: defined population terms one variable (age), members age also identical outcome value. Step 3: next step define population dynamics. requires defining four functions: fun_mortality(data, period) - needs return vector counts population cell fun_outmigration(data, period) - needs return vector counts population cell fun_coming_of_age(data, period) - needs return data frame new population cells column n fun_inmigration(data, period) - needs return data frame new population cells column n functions defined period--period. practice, often years. instance, mortality function return counts define many members population die within next period, population cell. -migration function defined way, just number people migrating population cell. coming age function -migration functions different: return data frame describes new population members added period--period. functions flexible enough describe complicated real-world population dynamics. first simple example, define functions way keeps population stable: don’t migration, mortality coming--age balances perfectly - every period 100 new members age 20 added, 100 members age 30 die. four functions optional, just define coming--age mortality function: ’re now ready start simulation: simulation result displays overview period, well summary total decomposition change. results year show indeed population stable: end every period population 1550, stable mean 0.38 - .e, 38% population supports increasing pensions. However, stability bit deceptive, everyone’s opinion actually keeps constantly changing. can see looking “Intraindividual change” component, +0.25. Roughly speaking, without population turnover, expected mean support increasing pensions increased quite bit, 63%. makes sense, ’ve designed simulation way support increases age. turn, younger people much less likely support increasing pensions. Hence, enter population, effect decreasing mean. true mortality. Hence, population turnover component negative. simulation, two components balance exactly, leading expected complete stabilty year--year mean. slightly complex setup, can add covariates simulation. instance, can introduce gender covariate. example, split former population two equally-distributed genders: also adjust population dynamics functions keep population stable. Note don’t depend gender (): reran simulation settings, get exact results . introduce slight variation, though, determine one gender actually fixed opinion support increasing pensions. gender behaves : Although overall mean doesn’t change time, decomposition results changed. men now contribute population turnover intraindividual change (women ), components half . previous scenario, overall outcome stable, intraindividual change well population turnover, cancelling . can also construct case shows intraindividual change, making outcome function dependent time (possibly covariates, ), age. expected, results show outcome increased 0.1 year--year, change due intraindividual change: Conversely, can also construct scenario change explained population turnover - older cohorts replaced younger cohorts. , define outcome function individual’s birth cohort: constants chosen two cases align. important thing outcome depends birth cohort (.e. cohort = year - age). results show exact dynamic – initial mean 0.3 increase 0.1 every year –, different explanations: Now change completely explained population turnover. one ever changes mind, social change occurs older cohorts low outcome values die younger cohorts higher outcome values follow. Hence, mortality coming--age contribute equally total change.

what: Decompose Social Change into Intraindividual Change and Population Turnover
title: Decompose Social Change into Intraindividual Change and Population Turnover
text: socialchange package provides methods decomposing aggregate-level social change components intraindividual change population turnover. Population turnover includes replacement individuals (mortality coming--age) migration. package implements event-based, aggregated-data, simulation-based decomposition approaches, well classical cohort replacement vs. intraindividual change (CR-IC) decompositions Age-Period-Cohort (APC) models.

title: Decompose Social Change into Intraindividual Change and Population Turnover

title: Decompose Social Change into Intraindividual Change and Population Turnover
text: basic example, look decomposition EU membership, whether population growth EU due intracountry changes population, due admission new countries. decomposition unit country. run decompose_events decomposition function, need two datasets: One dataset contains events, case dates countries entered exited EU. dataset included socialchange package: second dataset contains outcome want decompose point time change events, plus start end period interest. EU formed 1952, countries entered exited 1973, 1981, 1986, 1995, 2004, 2007, 2013, 2020. Countries, course, entered specific dates, population data provided yearly resolutions, assume error introduced using yearly resolution small. socialchange package also includes total population data countries world 1950 onwards, also includes required data: run decompose_events function, need ensure columns unit time parameters named , prepare data follows: Finally, run decomposition. specify two datasets, formula form Outcome ~ Unit + Time. also need specify end data, aggregation function. Often mean, case needs sum: outcome decomposition tells us intracountry changes population account 30% total population increase (80 million), additions one (Br)exit account 70% total population increase (184 million). also possible produce figure shows decomposition:

what: Cohort replacement vs. intracohort change decomposition — cr_ic
title: Cohort replacement vs. intracohort change decomposition — cr_ic
text: Decomposes total change outcome two periods cohort replacement (CR) intracohort change (IC) components. Four methods available:

title: Cohort replacement vs. intracohort change decomposition — cr_ic
code: cr_ic(data, formula, weight = NULL, model = NULL)

title: Cohort replacement vs. intracohort change decomposition — cr_ic
text: data data frame one row per respondent (one row per cohort-period cell `weight` supplied). formula formula form `outcome ~ period + cohort`. weight Name weight variable string. `NULL`, observations weighted equally. model Either model formula (e.g., `~ period * cohort`) fitted model object supports `predict()`. formula supplied fitted weighted `lm`. Required AD+ Model methods.

title: Cohort replacement vs. intracohort change decomposition — cr_ic
text: list class `cr_ic_decomposition` components: `summary` `data.table` summarising IC CR full period       method. `detailed` `data.table` year--year results       consecutive pair periods. `periods` Numeric vector periods found data. `cohort` `data.table` cohort-level IC CR contributions       (`model` supplied, otherwise `NULL`). `model` fitted supplied model object, `NULL`.

title: Cohort replacement vs. intracohort change decomposition — cr_ic
text: - **AD** (algebraic decomposition): Decomposes spanning cohorts IC   CR exactly. Entering exiting cohorts split assigned   entirely CR, Firebaugh notes "problematic". - **LD** (linear decomposition): Fits linear model `y ~ period + cohort`   uses coefficients attribute change. Handles entering/exiting   cohorts capture non-linearities produces residual. - **AD+**: Extends AD imputing missing outcome values entering/exiting   cohorts model supplied via `model`, applies algebraic   decomposition. Produces residual; quality depends model fit. - **Model**: Like AD+ replaces ** cohort-specific values (just   missing ones) model predictions. Acts form regularization,   can help small cohorts noisy estimates. AD LD always computed. AD+ Model require `model` argument.

title: Cohort replacement vs. intracohort change decomposition — cr_ic
text: Firebaugh, G. (1992). social change come ? Estimating relative contributions intra-cohort change population turnover. *Population Research Policy Review*, 11(1), 61–74.

what: Decompose social change from aggregated data — decompose_aggregated
title: Decompose social change from aggregated data — decompose_aggregated
text: Decomposes aggregate-level change intraindividual change population turnover components using microsimulation stacked cross-sectional data. Requires prediction function models outcome function age, period, covariates.

title: Decompose social change from aggregated data — decompose_aggregated
code: decompose_aggregated(   stacked_data,   fun_y,   cells = c(),   tol = 0.05,   weight = NULL,   population = NULL )  # S3 method for class 'social_change_decomp' plot(x, ...)

title: Decompose social change from aggregated data — decompose_aggregated
text: stacked_data Data frame columns age, period, y, plus optional cell identifiers. column n present data treated already aggregated cells; otherwise individual-level rows aggregated internally using weight. fun_y Prediction function taking (newdata) returning predicted outcome values cells Character vector additional cell identifier columns beyond age (e.g., "gender", "smoking") tol Maximum tolerated absolute deviation observed modeled period means, outcome's units (default 0.05). Checks fun_y reproduces observed period means; largest deviation exceeds tol, function errors. default suits outcomes roughly unit scale (e.g. proportions [0, 1]); set tol match outcomes another scale. weight Name weight column used aggregating individual-level data (ignored n present). Weights normalized within period sum period sample size aggregation, cell counts n (rounded sums normalized weights) reflect relative population structure rather raw sample sizes. preserves simulation tractability approximation: ideal approach use true population counts, generally unavailable survey data alone. population Optional data frame true cell counts n per cell period (columns period, age, cells identifiers, n). supplied, counts replace survey-derived cell counts population frame: drive event derivation weight modeled mean, fun_y continues supply every cell's outcome stacked_data used observed-mean / model-fit diagnostic. preferred input true population counts (e.g. census official statistics) available alongside survey data, sidesteps survey age-structure noise. n rounded whole counts microsimulation, rescale large frames (e.g. raw population counts millions) tractable per-period total first – relative cell structure matters. Periods cells need match survey exactly; cells absent survey still handled fun_y can predict outcome. x `social_change_decomp` object returned [decompose_aggregated()]. ... used.

title: Decompose social change from aggregated data — decompose_aggregated
text: S3 object class social_change_decomp components: summary: data.table decomposition components period (including       inmigration outmigration columns; print/plot show migration component       whichever non-zero) record: list detailed event records period transition

title: Decompose social change from aggregated data — decompose_aggregated
text: function estimates mortality, coming--age, net -migration period--period population differences within cells, uses microsimulation randomly order demographic events track contribution aggregate change. Unequal multi-year gaps periods supported: gap exceeds one year, entering cohort assigned specific calendar year within gap crosses minimum age, post-entry aging correctly attributed intraindividual change rather coming--age. default survey supplies cell counts outcomes. Supplying population decouples : population frame supplies cell counts n (hence inferred demographic events), fun_y supplies outcomes. reported modeled_mean weighted population frame, whereas observed_mean remains survey's observed mean, two lines may diverge survey population age structures differ. Within cell, survivor cohort shrinks periods loses people mortality, one grows gains people net -migration. net migration recovered: gross -migration separable deaths (survivor loss either), folded mortality reported -migration always zero. cell's net change attributed single event type sign: shrinking cell records mortality (concurrent -migration invisible) growing cell records net -migration (concurrent deaths folded ), offsetting flows within cell seen. New cohorts (minimum age earlier period) attribute growth coming--age; migration among entering cohorts modeled. noisy survey cells strategy relabels sampling fluctuation -migration mortality, inferred -migration meaningful population supplies true population frame, growing cohorts reflect genuine net immigration rather survey noise. Limitation: properly handle within-cell state transitions. Transition effects absorbed intraindividual change component.

title: socialchange 0.1.0
text: Initial release. decompose_events(): event-based decomposition discrete entry/exit populations. decompose_aggregated(): microsimulation-based decomposition aggregated cross-sectional data. sim_social_change(): forward simulation social change full event--event records. cr_ic(): cohort replacement vs. intracohort change decompositions (AD, LD, AD+, Model). apc(): Age-Period-Cohort models using orthogonal polynomial contrasts.
